text: The omentum is visible within the surgical field, likely being manipulated or repositioned during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the location of the umbilical hernia, situated below the previously mentioned anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon observes the yellow preperitoneal fat layer beneath the rectus muscle during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon maneuvers laterally to the trochar sites in the patient's left abdomen, positioning instruments for further exploration or intervention.
The surgeon incises the preperitoneal plane, revealing the underlying yellow preperitoneal fat beneath the rectus muscle.
The surgeon inserts a second instrument to assist in guiding the preperitoneal dissection, maneuvering within the surgical field to separate the preperitoneal fat from the underlying rectus muscle.
The surgeon uses Prograsma in the left hand and scissors in the right hand to manipulate tissues in the patient's left abdomen.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal fat layer, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the preperitoneal space, using a combination of instruments to separate tissue layers without cutting.
The surgeon applies counter traction with gentle pulling down and inward motion, manipulating the tissue layers during the hernia repair procedure.
The incisional hernia is visible above, having been de-peritonealized, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to increase the working space, aiming to reduce the hernia sac of the umbilical hernia.
The surgeon crosses the preperitoneal fat in the contralateral rectus, revealing a thinner layer of peritoneum beneath.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to gently buzz and push, hugging the anterior abdominal wall while carefully separating the peritoneum from its attachments above.
The surgeon reaches a lateral plane familiar to those performing transversus abdominis release, indicating the progression of dissection in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon attempts to create a large flap by carefully dissecting and separating tissue layers, aiming to maximize exposure in the surgical field.
The surgeon places mesh with adequate overlap over the hernia site, creating redundancy in the peritoneum to facilitate re-peritonealization of the mesh.
The surgeon demonstrates the mesh placement with adequate overlap over the hernia defect, ensuring proper coverage within the surgical field.
The resident closes the hernia defect using an oblock suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
The surgeon places a 13 by 15 centimeter sheet of flat mesh over the original defect site, ensuring it covers the area adequately for hernia repair.
The surgeon secures the flat mesh to the abdominal wall using cardinal tacks, ensuring the mesh lays flat against the tissue.
The surgeon re-peritonealizes the mesh, covering it with the peritoneum to ensure proper integration and protection within the abdominal cavity.
The video shows an obvious defect in the peritoneum, highlighting the area that requires repair.
The surgeon utilizes the falciform ligament to assist in closing the defect, demonstrating a technique that leverages this anatomical structure for effective closure.
The surgeon incises the falciform ligament to mobilize it, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon uses a sheet of tissue to cover holes in the peritoneum, ensuring the mesh is no longer exposed.
